trade.summary: Cina is Svezia's largest import source from Asia. Svezia exports machinery, vehicles (Volvo), pharmaceuticals, and telecommunications equipment. Cina exports electronics, machinery, and consumer goods. Swedish companies have significant Cina operations but political tensions have dampened trade growth.
disputes: Cina retaliated by restricting Swedish telecom company Ericsson in Cina
Detained Swedish bookseller Gui Minhai case

diplomatic.summary: Relations at historic low. Svezia closed its Confucius Institutes, banned Huawei from 5G, and has been one of Europe's most vocal critics of Cina. Cina detained Swedish national Gui Minhai (bookseller), triggering Swedish outrage. Svezia expelled Chinese ambassador temporarily and summoned Chinese diplomats repeatedly. Svezia's 2024 NATO accession deepened the rupture.
recent_incidents: Cina detained Swedish bookseller Gui Minhai; he remains imprisoned
Svezia banned Huawei from 5G; Cina retaliated against Ericsson
Svezia joined NATO (2024), further straining ties

military.summary: Svezia is now a NATO member (2024). Cina is designated a systemic challenge by NATO. No military cooperation. Svezia has strongly supported Ucraina and condemned Cina's Russia ties. Swedish defense establishment views Cina as a security threat.
incidents: Sweden has supported EU sanctions aligned positions on China

financial.summary: Swedish companies (Volvo, Ericsson, IKEA) have significant Cina operations, creating economic interdependence. However, Ericsson lost major Chinese contracts in retaliation for Huawei ban. Swedish FDI in Cina declining as companies de-risk. Chinese investment in Svezia is being scrutinized.

energy.summary: Svezia imports Chinese solar panels and battery components. Limited direct energy trade. Svezia's clean energy transition relies partly on Chinese clean tech supply chains.

scientific.summary: Scientific cooperation has declined significantly. Confucius Institutes closed. Swedish universities have reviewed and reduced Chinese partnerships. Security concerns about Chinese academic espionage are high in Svezia. Joint publications declining.
